sentences.text: Well, in about the third day after this little war that we had to do with the-- to the Red Army, I had to report, because I already knew, because there were all kinds of wandering people, people around.
And when the-- the soldiers, patrols, if they caught you, you had to be-- you had to be on safe ground to do what you are.
So I inquired and I got information that such and such a place only [INAUDIBLE] away from my safe house, there is a district Soviet command.
And a major was there, and with Therese, the daughter of the Schandl family.
She spoke, since she spoke Russian, she spoke German, she spoke English.
And she interpreted to me in English.
And so when I introduced myself, you see, in English, she translated that in-- in Russian-- in to-- to-- to the major.
So I was-- I was checked out, and I told them my-- my-- my code-- code number, ME6.
And in-- in-- in five days' time, they checked it out from-- from-- from Debrecen, because that was the allied military-- military mission already.
In five days' time, was checked out, and they put me in a-- and they let me go home to the Schandls for-- for-- for one day and come back, and then I never saw-- saw them again.
And they keep me for a couple days.
They even took me over to the Transdanubia to see the battlefield, the German and the Soviet battlefield.
And then-- then-- then they took me-- took me out to Debrecen, to the what you call.
And from there-- from there, well, a lot of things has happened in-- in-- in a few days.
We did American plane, because there was the Americans, the British, the French, and the Soviets military commission there.
And we did American plane.
They flew us-- flew us back to-- back to Bari in Italy.
From there, they flew us back not there enough, into Siena, an ancient, ancient Italian town.
There we spent, I don't know, 10, 11 days or something, or maybe-- maybe more.
And then finally we got our order, and we took a train down to Naples.
There was a British ship there waiting, waiting for-- there was some Canadian troops there who-- who were the rear guards, because the Canadians fought there in earlier years.
And then we came back to-- back to England.
And then they discharged me, and my [INAUDIBLE] was with me from the British Secret Service there.
And then on a-- I forget the name of the ship that they-- we sailed back to Canada, and here I got my--
--My discharge paper from the Canadian Army.
No, no, not in Budapest, because when I separated from Mrs.
Szenes-- Mrs.
Tigany, that was the-- from the doctor's wife, I heard when I was in the other room, when she was telling to Mrs.-- Mrs.
Tigany, this doctor's wife, that Therese-- Therese?
Yeah, her name was also Therese.
I heard that Hannah Szenes is back in the country.
Gee, I got a shock.
But I wasn't sure because Szenes is so common name.
I wasn't so sure.
But only-- only years after I got a Hungarian-- a Hungarian language book from Israel, from-- from Joe [?
Gershman, ?] the editor of [?
Wochenblatt. ?] He got it from somebody from Israel, this Hungarian book, and we were printing in the same print shop in Toronto here.
And were in the same editor room, and [INAUDIBLE] correction and so on.
Said, well, it is a Hungarian book, from where I got it, from-- from Israel.
And I looked, looked it over, and in about three sentences, her name was mentioned.
Oh my gosh.
It was the first time in about-- well, two, maybe three years-- three years later.
And I knew that-- and in a book that just disappears somewhere.
But that's when I was sure that somewhere, she, like many, many, of us, she's one of the-- one of the disappeared person.
Then it took a long, long-- many long years then, then through the new editors of the Menorah, I got in touch with the-- with the [INAUDIBLE], rabbi.
Yeah.
And then-- then I heard about-- about Mrs.
Szenes, and [INAUDIBLE] that they're living in Israel.
And then I met some-- met artists, Jewish comrades who were from Poland, [INAUDIBLE], some [INAUDIBLE] who lived in Israel, and they were in-- in a Communist Party in Israel.
And they told me that the Jews and all of us are together, the Communist Party.
There is only one Communist Party.
And through one of these comrades in the UJPO here, I-- I got hold of-- of George's address somehow.
And I sent him a letter, and it's still here.
And I never even opened it.
George Szenes.
George is Hannah's brother.
The brother.
Yeah.

And I-- I read-- read about, or heard about from these people who came from Israel-- not Hungarians, but artists, came-- came from Israel, heard about-- they mentioned, when I talked about my experience, and somebody took-- you must know Mrs.
Szenes.
No, I don't know Mrs.
Szenes, but I knew Hannah Szenes.
Do you know Hannah Szenes personally?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, so I just-- just heard about Hannah, and then George, that was there.
And I just read in the press somewhere when-- when she died.
And I have a picture somewhere with-- who was the president-- prime minister, the lady, Polish lady, [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
Golda Meir, yeah.
I-- that I have a picture together.
Everything's somewhere in a book here.
I tried.
Then I asked for the exact address of the Communist Party in Tel Aviv.
And I was given the address.
So I wrote an English letter, because they told me, just write, write in English, because the English is just as-- so much [INAUDIBLE] as Hebrew.
And wrote them an English letter, and-- and-- and put a Hungarian-written letter to Mrs.
Szenes.

And I called her my mother, on my knee and all my-- all my respect and everything else that you are-- you are the mother of Hannah Szenes, and this is who I am what I am and so on, and asking-- asking the communists there to forward this letter-- letter to Mrs.
Szenes, because I know-- I've heard from comrades here, that you know her, that's she's-- she's an outstanding person.
And George, in Israel.
But never-- never got a reply.
No.
And how I got the address of George, then I wrote him a letter, and then I have it here.
I'm going to show it to you.
And an address, and there is a stamp on it.
Or you're going to read it off to me, or-- but anyway, it came back, the letter from George.
No, never thought of but I had another contact.
Mrs.-- Mrs.
Szenes' cousin is a wife of Erno Sass, who was a lawyer-- who is a lawyer.

And after my-- my exposure on Budapest-- Budapest television, all these people came, telephoned, and letters, and the messages and so-- to the station, to the party office, to the whole Federation of Hungarians.
Everybody wanted to see that-- speak to this guy-- mostly-- mostly Jewish people, and the cousins of-- cousins of Hannah.
This is Sass, his name-- S-- S-A-S-S.
Sass [INAUDIBLE].
And I looked him up, because he called and so on, and selected a day and went to them.
And he was-- he went-- he visited Israel once or twice-- twice a year, and wrote a certain time-- wrote a long, long letter to Mrs.
Szenes again, and so on.
And he handed the letter over.
But somehow-- somehow they weren't sure.
Because when I had the television interview in Budapest, the Tel Aviv radio denounced me.
Who this man coming 20 or 25 or 30 years later, coming in from Canada into Hungary, and proclaims himself, and reveals himself and claims that Hannah Szenes was his fiance?
He's an imposter.
He's a crook.
And they used a name-- in Israel, a Hungarian national used his name that he was in Budapest at the time when I was on the television.
And that man-- and I think found out from [INAUDIBLE]-- that man protested the radio, on radio, to distract this because he never heard my name.
He never knew-- heard anything ever about me.
Why they use his name, this [INAUDIBLE]?
But no, no.
I don't understand.
This man.
This Israeli man--
Yeah.
Well, he simply-- simply said, because they-- they used his name on the radio, when they denounced me, that this so and so person--
--was in Budapest when this Canadian-- Canadian Andrew Durovecz was on television and claimed Hannah Szenes to himself.
Against the Tel Aviv radio, that they used his name-- that they used his name.
But not really.
No, no, no, but Sass spoke to him.
And [INAUDIBLE] to, if he ever meets me again to apologize, because he had nothing to do with me, and he demanded from the radio station that they distract it, because he doesn't know me.
But they never did.
But we had another incident where I was in a Hungarian military history institute, a history institute, and they were interviewing me, a woman about your size and so on.
A colonel-- he was second in command.

And to be sure that they wanted to document everything, she sent down a message to Israel to the military institute there, military history, and the-- they reply was this-- your man-- your man is an imposter.
Hannah Szenes was never in British service.
He was sent there by the Zionists, the Palestine Zionists at the time to liberate the Jews-- this kind of nonsense.
And I had to make a point about this in my book, you see.
They even denied that he was in British-- British service, that he volunteered.

By Christ, what it took for the British to find her and find me, and the [INAUDIBLE] people selected, people that trained them, with all the expenses and so on, and this-- this [INAUDIBLE] people they they-- she wasn't in-- even in a British service.
No.
No.
I tried to.
I was really anxious, because Palgi and Goldstein-- Goldstein they-- landed in Kasztner's office, you see, and they inquired about Hannah Szenes-- was she reported to you.
They didn't know about Kasztner.
And Kasztner used them for a while, [INAUDIBLE], and finally he threw them-- not only them, but there were other-- the two artists.
He threw them to the-- to the-- to the Gestapo.
And one of them escaped from a train-- on a prisoners train, he escaped from the train.
And Palgi and Goldstein, who was-- who used to play football against each other-- he was from [INAUDIBLE].
And then somehow they were able to escape in a mishmash somehow, and I wanted to speak to him, because he was the closest to-- closest to Hannah.
And I wanted to go to Israel.
But mind you, I was warned by this communist there, and-- and-- and-- and in-- in-- in-- in Budapest by [INAUDIBLE], where the way the situation, the [?
Haganah, ?] he get hold of you and you never, never returned from it.
Then the situation was so bad there-- a bunch of mad men.
And some of them, mind you, were British trained, in the same service, and and then-- then they joined the Haganah, and so on.
But also, this one who landed in Toronto, they told me-- we looked.
Your kind of stories, they're so nationalist, so Zionist and so extremist.

And so when you learned [INAUDIBLE] that this kind of story-- and oh, when I came back after that radio, television interview, two weeks later, I had a telephone call from the Jewish Defense League here-- here in Canada, right on the telephone there, and they-- they threatened me.
And I told them, all right, come, you son of a bitch [INAUDIBLE].
Who-- where you are and what I am, and my story is what I, what I-- I told to Hungary and to the world, whoever wants to know it.
And I'm not going to back down.
But you want to meet me?
But under one condition-- we go in a meeting in [INAUDIBLE] where nobody else see us.
And then they-- gave up.
What did they want from you?
Well, they-- they cursed me, too.
Who are-- you are-- you are a vagabond.
You are this and that.
You claim-- you claim you served-- you were the boyfriend and who knows what to Hannah Szenes.
Hannah Szenes was a Zionist hero, and so on and so forth, some [INAUDIBLE].
So that's-- [INAUDIBLE] Jewish Defense League here is an extreme Zionist gang.
Sometimes I met them at the-- at the [INAUDIBLE].
That is not here, but someday I will go [INAUDIBLE].
Well, not exactly.
I was-- I was on probation, even by my party.
And-- and my staff, really-- editor board and the leadership of the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE].
From-- I arrived back in in the beginning of June 1945.
I got two weeks or three weeks furlough.
Went out west to see my family and so on.
And then when I came back, nothing said-- absolutely nothing-- not even Tim Buck, you see, who was responsible, who were recruiting.
And [INAUDIBLE], because after all, we were with the British intelligence, you see-- in cahoot with the British intelligence.
And we were in contact with that goes back centuries, at least 300 centuries, the [INAUDIBLE] and they knew what kind of [INAUDIBLE].
And we were-- we were rubbing shoulders with hem.
And then Tim Buck went back to-- went back to Hungary three times.
And then I was checked out in Budapest, in [NON-ENGLISH], in Prague, in Moscow, because I was in contact with the-- with the [?
Malinowski ?] and [INAUDIBLE] through-- through what you call runners and furnished them with information inside-- inside, and encircle city, and with very great success.
We were able to bust out, to-- to shred the German mobile radio station.
[INAUDIBLE] And I gave him exact.
And they had artillery-- my God, they blasted it to slivers.
But I never met any of these people, really.
Yeah.
Then I-- I just took a-- took up some job.
As a matter of fact, there was two Polish Jews here who were in contact with a Hungarian plumber, and they were builders, you see.
They're doing these alterations you know.
And John told me, the Hungarian plumber-- I lived in his house-- these people, they wanted and they want to help.
And [INAUDIBLE] you want to work?
Oh yes, because I had-- I had nothing, you see.
And then came the tobacco harvest.
Then I went to the Hungarian farmers.
Then and there I did [INAUDIBLE].
Anyway, I went out in the second-- no, the last-- [INAUDIBLE] in August, when they started it.
And it was coming to the end of September-- to September, when the manager of our paper, just on a Sunday afternoon, just walked into the farm where I was so-- I was [INAUDIBLE].
And pack up and go.
Hold it now-- I have a couple more days-- a couple more days, and I don't want to leave this because we were good workers, and they were good-- good bosses and so on.
The Mrs.
Made good food and so on, and [INAUDIBLE].
I want to finish.
I don't want to leave them at the last moment.
Oh yes.
I get disability pension, yeah.
Well, this area, this 200-acre farm, you see it from here, that [INAUDIBLE] dairy, that was the farm there, the [INAUDIBLE] farm, the name of it.
They had a road in from-- from-- highway-- highway 7.
And it was their [INAUDIBLE] farm road.
And they had a mink farm or a fox farm or something-- something there.
But then the government bought this place up for [INAUDIBLE] settlement.
For soldiers-- experience from the First World War.
When veterans came back, they had no place to go, meaning there was a lot of trouble.
They had problems with them.
Then they demonstrated and stormed the city hall and so on to give us something, after all.
And we don't want this after the Second World War, so this here they-- but not very many veterans.
You see, when we-- when we came back, somehow then sooner than later, everybody formed something.
And there were about, I don't know, maybe 20 people, [INAUDIBLE], and now there are three of us-- myself, my neighbor here, and one on River Bend Road there, an old man.
All my neighbors, all when I moved out here in the '80s-- '78, March '78-- my God, this is March.
How many years is it now, 26, 27 years?
They all were all veterans at that time.
My God.
Yes.
Well, my wife is at-- that's another sad, sad story, that.
One of the reasons, besides her intelligence and so on, she resembles Hannah so much.
And-- but then the family set up-- my father in law and my sister in law, especially my-- well, both, her father and her sister, [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yes.
Yeah, she's always in front, yeah.
Oh, yes, yes.

What angers me, that her name is very well known in the Jewish community in Toronto, but instead of her, they erected a statue or a monument to Wallenberg, a CIA agent whose task was, if he could have pushed it through, there would have been a third world war starting right in Hungary, because they wanted to stop the Red Army, [INAUDIBLE], not to let the Bolsheviks into Western-- Western Europe.
That was his assignment from Dulles, who was sitting in Switzerland.
The billionaire-- you read the stories they wrote.
When I saw it again, that they erected the statue for him in London-- but [INAUDIBLE] Bales Park [INAUDIBLE].
Bales Park there.
I wanted to go there to print a leaflet, but what the hell?
I haven't got so-- so much spare money to hand out the leaflet and expose this-- expose this guy.
Well, I-- I was there.
My contact was Karl Schandl Jr., who was an officer in the Hungarian army, and from the-- from the officer staff, the high command of the Hungarian army, [INAUDIBLE] dispersed information about it.
Before I heard it from him, I heard rumors already, because of the Swedish-- Swedish embassy was so close, that this man is circulating around.
He's-- he's pretending that he's-- he's saving Jews and so on.
But he's doing dirty things.
He's going to the rich Jews and collecting money and-- and jewelry and so on and so forth, and he share it with the-- with the-- with the SS and so on.
So there was a confusion about the man.
But somebody already-- already suspected from somewhere, because there were other people, probably, in the high command that they knew about-- about Wallenberg.
And these things went around.
And when I got this information from this Schandl, Karl Schandl, when he started to tell-- oh, hold it.
I know.
I already heard something about this.
Now tell me your story.
I told him mine, what I told you now, that the guy is circulating [INAUDIBLE].
You tell me.
And then I heard it right from there.
And he-- and he was honest about it, that they wanted to stop the Red Army and move the Americans into Hungary.
And it could have been-- it could have been-- well, nobody can say anything.
Well, after all, they are allies, and the Americans got there first.
But the thing was that Patton, George Patton, this hardened American gangster.
He was a real conniver.
He had an army, commanding the American army in Italy, and that was the plan, that he's going to lead the American army into Budapest before the Red Army gets there.
And it would have been-- boom, the start of the third-- because that was the-- would have been busting up the great alliance.
And we were just half split away from Hitler winning this war.
And I have to make this statement and make it to many Jews-- well, look-- because there are reactionary Jews and so on-- watch this.

If it wasn't for the Red Army, apart from what happened in the Soviet Union, what you say about Stalin and so on and so forth, and Bolshevism-- if it wasn't for the Red Army, there wouldn't be one Jew on this Earth today, because Hitler and his mad dog gang would have exterminated you, because we were just a step away from-- from the invasion of America.
And because of Churchill and Stalin, especially two-- two person, because Roosevelt was an illiterate and ignorant about European-- European politics.
But it was Stalin and Churchill who knew what they are doing.
And they organized everything it was possible-- had to put into-- into motion to stop Hitler.
People don't know it.
Those who were there and so-- and even Jews who escaped and they are survivors of the Holocaust, many of them have forgotten about the [INAUDIBLE].
I know, I know.
And I ask many of them, show me one.
Show me one who was saved by Wallenberg.

When he talks about that Wallenberg-- Wallenberg opened up the safe house and put the Jews there-- the Nazis were putting them in a yellow star-- the yellow star [INAUDIBLE], and they pin it on Wallenberg.
He was there for-- for the purpose.
And I have it in-- it's collected.
Show it to you.
